slides.title: One Assignment, Two Realities
slides.subtitle: Why the same homework costs two students different amounts — and the characteristics that decide who gets a fair shot online
slides.footerLeft: AP Computer Science Principles · College Board CED (Effective Fall 2023)
slides.script: Topic 5.2, the Digital Divide — one day, one learning objective. Topic 5.1 asked whether a computing innovation helps or harms; today's question is sharper: who even gets to use it? The lesson opens with two fictional students receiving the identical assignment and paying wildly different prices to complete it. By the end of the period, students can name the characteristics that access varies along, who the divide affects, and who can act on it — exactly what LO IOC-1.C asks them to describe.

slides.heading: By the End of Today You Will...

items.text: Describe the digital divide and how access varies by socioeconomic, geographic, and demographic characteristics and between countries (LO IOC-1.C)
Explain how the divide affects groups and individuals, and how individuals, organizations, and governments widen or narrow it (LO IOC-1.C)

slides.script: Two targets, splitting LO IOC-1.C down its natural seam. First, the anatomy of the divide: what the term means and the socioeconomic, geographic, and demographic characteristics — plus between-countries gaps — along which Internet access varies. Second, the people side: the divide can affect whole groups and single individuals, it raises issues of equity, access, and influence, and it is moved by actions at three levels — individual, organization, government. Exam items on this topic almost always test one of those two halves.

slides.prompt: Two students get tonight's homework: watch a 20-minute video, research the topic online, and upload a slideshow by Friday. Maya has fiber Internet at home. Jordan has a phone with 2 GB of data a month — and the nearest library Wi-Fi is a 40-minute bus ride away.
slides.subprompt: List every step of the assignment where it costs Jordan something it does not cost Maya — minutes, dollars, data, or sleep. Star where the gap starts.
slides.directions: Write your list of steps. Be ready to defend your starred choice.
slides.script: A few minutes, on your own — walk the assignment step by step through both students' evenings. Maya streams the video at dinner; Jordan must ration 2 GB, so the video alone could eat a tenth of the month, and the research and the upload each bill him again. Most lists surface bus fare, closing-time deadlines, homework done on a phone keyboard, and sleep lost to travel — the same task, priced differently by circumstances neither student chose. Students are inventing the digital divide as a lived experience before it gets a name; every later slide will pin College Board vocabulary onto costs they found themselves. Keep the lists visible — they get sorted into categories at the first Stop and Think.

slides.heading: What You'll Be Filling In
slides.subheading: Your Guided Notes have 3 sections that match today's lesson. Green = CB required.

sections.title: What the Divide Is
sections.description: The definition, plus the socioeconomic, geographic, and demographic characteristics access varies along (EK IOC-1.C.1–C.3).

sections.title: Who It Affects, Who Can Act
sections.description: Equity, access, and influence — and the individuals, organizations, and governments that move the gap (EK IOC-1.C.4–C.5).

sections.title: Deep Dive: More Than On or Off
sections.description: Speed, data caps, devices, and skills — why access is a sliding scale, not a switch.

slides.note: Fill in your Guided Notes as we go. Absent or lost your packet? A digital copy lives on the Topic 5.2 Guided Notes page at apcsexamprep.com.
slides.script: Today's Guided Notes map: three sections. Two are green and CB-required — the divide's definition and dimensions, then who it affects and who can act on it. The third is the blue deep dive on connection quality and skills: beyond the exam, but it explains why handing out devices alone rarely closes a gap. Packets stay open all period; a digital copy lives on the Topic 5.2 Guided Notes page at apcsexamprep.com for anyone absent.

slides.label: What the Divide Is
slides.kicker: The Definition and Its Dimensions · Learning Objective IOC-1.C
slides.script: Section one. The bell ringer produced a pile of unequal costs; this section gives that inequality its official name and its dimensions. The College Board wants three characteristic types plus the between-countries clause — and the point that the divide can land on a whole group or on one person.

slides.heading: Key Vocabulary (LO IOC-1.C)

terms.definition: Differing access to computing devices and the Internet, based on socioeconomic, geographic, or demographic characteristics (EK IOC-1.C.2).
Giving every person the identical resource — one hotspot per household — whether or not it fits their situation (EK IOC-1.C.4 contrast term).
Matching resources to need so everyone gets a real chance to participate — the fairness issue the divide raises, alongside access and influence (EK IOC-1.C.4).

slides.script: Three terms anchor the topic. The digital divide is the CED's phrase for differing access to computing devices and the Internet, and the definition itself lists the three characteristic types — socioeconomic, geographic, demographic. Then the pair students routinely blur: equality hands everyone the identical thing, equity matches the resource to the need. Jordan and a fiber-connected classmate both receiving one free hotspot is equality; Jordan getting the hotspot he lacks is equity. The exam raises this through EK IOC-1.C.4, which names equity as one of the issues the divide creates.

slides.heading: The Dimensions of the Divide
bullets: Internet access varies between socioeconomic, geographic, and demographic characteristics, as well as between countries (EK IOC-1.C.1).
Socioeconomic: income decides who can pay for devices and monthly plans. Geographic: where you live decides what infrastructure reaches you.
Demographic: age, language, disability, and other population traits shape who gets — and comfortably uses — a connection.
The divide can affect both groups and individuals: an entire county with no fiber, or one student in a fully connected city (EK IOC-1.C.3).
slides.script: Here is the anatomy. Access varies along three characteristic types — socioeconomic, geographic, demographic — and, zooming out, between countries; exam items hand you a scenario and expect the right label back. Sort the bell ringer: Jordan's 2 GB cap is socioeconomic, the 40-minute ride to Wi-Fi is geographic, and a grandparent who owns a tablet but never opens it is demographic. The last bullet is the scale rule from EK IOC-1.C.3: the divide is just as real when it isolates one person as when it covers a region — students tend to only count the big version.

capture.q: Access differs within a nation and also ______.
capture.a: from one nation to the next across the globe

capture.q: A gap counts as geographic when it is driven by ______.
capture.a: where someone lives and which wires reach that address

capture.q: The divide is about differing access to ______.
capture.a: hardware plus a working connection to the online world

slides.heading: “It's Only About Poor Countries”
slides.misconception: The digital divide means the gap between wealthy and developing nations — inside a rich, wired country, everyone has basically the same access.
slides.whyItHappens: News coverage usually shows the divide at the between-countries scale, so the local version — two students in the same zip code — stays invisible.
slides.correction: Access varies between countries AND between socioeconomic, geographic, and demographic groups inside one (EK IOC-1.C.1), and the issues of equity, access, and influence arise both globally and locally (EK IOC-1.C.4). The divide runs through classrooms, not just borders.
slides.example: Maya and Jordan share a school, a town, and an assignment — and still sit on opposite sides of the divide. The between-countries gap is real, but so is the one inside a single class roster.
slides.script: This is the misconception the quiz will punish hardest. The between-countries clause is genuinely in EK IOC-1.C.1, so students are not wrong to think globally — they are wrong to think ONLY globally. The CED doubles down in C.4: equity, access, and influence issues arise both globally AND locally. The bell ringer is the counterexample students already own: Maya and Jordan live in the same town in a wired nation, and the divide sits between their two desks. Any exam option that confines the divide to national borders is a distractor.

slides.heading: Stop and Think — Name the Dimensions
slides.subheading: These map to LO IOC-1.C. Complete on your Guided Notes.
prompts: Sort your bell-ringer list into the three characteristic types — socioeconomic, geographic, demographic. Which bucket holds the most of Jordan's costs, and which cost fits no bucket neatly?
A mountain valley has no provider at all; one student in a wired suburb has no home plan. Explain how BOTH cases are the digital divide, and name who is affected in each.
Define the digital divide in one sentence without using the word 'Internet' — then check that your definition still covers devices.
slides.directions: Answer in complete sentences. Then check yourself with the matching CFUs on the Topic 5.2 page.
slides.script: Five minutes, full sentences on the Guided Notes. One: the sort matters more than the totals — the 2 GB cap is socioeconomic, the bus ride is geographic, and sleep lost is a cost cascading off both, which is the point: real gaps stack. Two: the valley is a group with a geographic gap; the suburban student is an individual with a socioeconomic one — EK IOC-1.C.3 counts both. Three: a strong definition says differing access to computing devices and the network, based on money, place, or population traits. A few students will be called on before section two.

slides.label: Who It Affects, Who Can Act
slides.kicker: Equity, Access, Influence — and Who Can Act · LO IOC-1.C
slides.script: Section two — the people side of the LO. The divide is not just a map of missing cables; it decides who participates, who is heard, and who shapes what gets built online. And it is not weather: it moves when individuals, organizations, and governments act. This section ends with students diagnosing five scenarios themselves.

slides.heading: Two Sides of the Same Gap
slides.subheading: Who the divide lands on — and who can move it (EK IOC-1.C.3–C.5).

left.title: Who it affects
left.definition: Both groups and individuals feel the gap — and its fairness costs
examples: A group: a whole district where homes lack broadband
An individual: one student uploading homework from a parking lot
Issues raised: equity, access, and influence — globally and locally

right.title: Who can act
right.definition: Actions at three levels widen or narrow the divide
examples: Individuals: coach a relative, donate a device, share a connection
Organizations: low-cost plans, refurbished laptops, community Wi-Fi
Governments: broadband buildout, subsidies, public-access policy

slides.footer: AP TIP: distractors love handing the whole divide to governments — the CED names individuals, organizations, AND governments as forces on it (EK IOC-1.C.5).
slides.script: Left side: who the divide lands on. EK IOC-1.C.3 says groups and individuals, and EK IOC-1.C.4 names what it costs them — equity, access, and influence, both globally and locally. Influence is the sleeper: people who are not online are absent from the forums, forms, and datasets where decisions get made, so their needs stop being visible. Right side: who can move it. EK IOC-1.C.5 assigns agency at three levels, and every level has real moves — a neighbor teaching a neighbor counts just as much on the exam as a national fiber program. The trap in the footer shows up as a quiz item today.

capture.q: The divide can hit an entire population or ______.
capture.a: a single household on an otherwise connected street

capture.q: The three levels that can move the divide are ______.
capture.a: single people, groups such as companies or nonprofits, and public authorities

capture.q: Being offline also means losing ______ over what happens online.
capture.a: a say in the decisions and conversations

slides.heading: Diagnose the Gap
slides.context: Five snapshots. For each: name the characteristic driving the gap, then one actor who could narrow it (EK IOC-1.C.1, C.5).
rows: A rural county where no provider offers wired broadband
A family in a wired city cannot afford any monthly plan
Organization — offer a verified low-cost plan
Seniors own donated tablets but rarely go online
Individual — volunteer to coach a neighbor
Students in one nation stream lessons; in another, most homes have no connection
A student's only Internet is a capped 2 GB phone plan
slides.script: Five snapshots, two diagnoses each — this is the exam skill for the whole topic. Row one: no infrastructure reaches the county, so the gap is geographic, and infrastructure at that scale is classic government action. Row two: the wires exist but the bill blocks the family — socioeconomic, and an organization's low-cost plan attacks the price directly. Row three is the one students miss: devices are present, so the barrier is demographic — age and confidence — and one patient individual can move it. Row four is the between-countries clause; row five is Jordan, connected on paper but capped in practice, still a socioeconomic gap. Students fill the blanked cells before answers are revealed.

slides.heading: Deep Dive: More Than On or Off
bullets: The AP definition asks WHO has access; researchers also ask HOW GOOD it is — speed, data caps, and the device it runs on change what a connection can do.
'Underconnected' users are online but limited: a phone-only plan can stream a lesson yet makes writing an essay or building a project painful.
Skills and confidence form a second-level divide: two people with identical connections can get very different value from them.
None of this is tested by the AP exam — but it explains why handing out devices alone rarely closes a gap.
slides.script: A deep dive beyond the CED. The exam treats access mostly as have or have-not, but researchers describe a spectrum: a 2 GB phone plan, a shared laptop on rural satellite, and home fiber are all 'access' with wildly different ceilings. The word for Jordan's situation is underconnected — online, but only for the lightest uses. And even identical connections split again on skills and confidence, which is why the senior-center tablets in the worked table stayed boxed. The practical payoff: real digital-inclusion programs bundle hardware, service, and training, not devices alone.

capture.q: A phone-only student is technically online yet still ______.
capture.a: boxed out of heavy work like long documents and editing

capture.q: Second-level divides come from differences in ______, not equipment.
capture.a: know-how and comfort with the tools

slides.heading: Stop and Think — Who Moves the Gap?
slides.subheading: These map to LO IOC-1.C. Complete on your Guided Notes.
prompts: For Jordan's 2 GB situation, propose one action at each level — individual, organization, government — and star the action you would fund first.
A friend says fixing the divide is 'the government's job, full stop.' Use one row of the diagnose-the-gap table to show what that view leaves out.
The town buys every household the identical hotspot. Using equity vs. equality, explain who this fix still leaves behind.
slides.directions: Answer in complete sentences. Then check yourself with the matching CFUs on the Topic 5.2 page.
slides.script: Six minutes, full sentences. One: a classmate sharing notes offline is individual action, the school lending hotspots is organizational, a state broadband subsidy is governmental — any defensible star earns credit, the levels matter. Two: the senior-center row breaks the friend's rule — no law builds confidence with a tablet; a volunteer does, and EK IOC-1.C.5 names all three levels on purpose. Three: identical hotspots are equality; the farmhouse outside coverage and the senior who needs training are still stranded, which is exactly the equity issue EK IOC-1.C.4 raises. Answers are reviewed before the traps slide.

slides.heading: Common AP Traps
slides.subheading: Three ways Topic 5.2 loses points on the exam — one minute now, real points in May.

strategies.name: “It's just about money”
strategies.text: Income is one axis, not the whole divide. The CED adds geographic AND demographic characteristics, plus between-countries gaps — a wealthy farm with no lines still counts.

strategies.name: Groups AND individuals
strategies.text: Distractors claim the divide needs a whole region or population. EK IOC-1.C.3 says it can affect both groups and individuals — one offline student in a wired city counts.

strategies.name: Three actor levels
strategies.text: Options that hand the divide to governments alone are wrong. EK IOC-1.C.5 credits individuals, organizations, AND governments — a tablet class or a low-cost plan moves it too.

slides.script: One minute, three traps. Trap one: students collapse the divide into income, but the CED's list is longer — an option that explains every gap with money misses geographic and demographic cases like the unserved farm or the untrained senior. Trap two: scale — EK IOC-1.C.3 explicitly covers the single student, so 'it only applies to whole regions' is a distractor. Trap three: agency — when a question shows a teacher, a company, and a state all acting, the CED-aligned answer credits all three levels, not just the government. Students write each trap in their own words in the packet.

slides.heading: The Digital Divide, in One Slide
points: The digital divide = differing access to computing devices and the Internet, based on socioeconomic, geographic, or demographic characteristics (EK IOC-1.C.2).
Access varies along those characteristics AND between countries (EK IOC-1.C.1).
It can affect both groups and individuals — a region, or one student (EK IOC-1.C.3).
It raises issues of equity, access, and influence, both globally and locally (EK IOC-1.C.4).
It is affected by the actions of individuals, organizations, and governments (EK IOC-1.C.5).
slides.next: Topic 5.3, Computing Bias: access is only half the fairness story — what happens when the software itself treats groups of people differently?
slides.script: The whole topic in one slide, and it is five sentences long. The divide is differing access to computing devices and the Internet; it runs along socioeconomic, geographic, and demographic lines and between countries; it lands on groups and on individuals; it raises equity, access, and influence issues globally and locally; and it moves when individuals, organizations, and governments act. Next class opens with the Topic 5.2 quiz, then Topic 5.3 asks how bias gets into the software itself. Before packing up, students run the exit check below — any unchecked box is tonight's review target on the Topic 5.2 page. Guided Notes are collected at the end of class.
ican: define the digital divide and the characteristics it runs along (LO IOC-1.C)
explain how the divide affects groups and individuals, globally and locally (LO IOC-1.C)
name actions at all three levels — individual, organization, government (LO IOC-1.C)

quiz.title: Topic 5.2 — Digital Divide — Quiz
quiz.instructions: Choose the single best answer. 7 questions, 12 minutes, no notes.

questions.stem: A telehealth service finds that patients in a rural county rarely join video appointments, while patients in the nearby city join reliably. Investigation shows no broadband provider serves most of the county. Per EK IOC-1.C.1, which characteristic of the digital divide does this illustrate?
options: A demographic characteristic — the county's patients are older than the city's patients
A geographic characteristic — where patients live determines what infrastructure reaches them
A socioeconomic characteristic — the county's patients cannot afford the service that is offered
A between-countries gap — the two regions count as separate nations for access purposes
questions.rationale: EK IOC-1.C.1 lists geographic location among the characteristics Internet access varies along, and the stem's evidence — no provider serves the county — is infrastructure-by-location, exactly option B. A invents an age difference the stem never mentions. C contradicts the finding: there is no available service for the patients to afford. D misreads two regions of one nation as different countries; the between-countries clause compares nations, not neighboring counties.

questions.stem: Which statement best defines the 'digital divide' as the term is used in the CED (EK IOC-1.C.2)?
options: The gap in processing speed between older computing devices and newer ones
The difference between wired connections and wireless connections to the Internet
Differing access to computing devices and the Internet, based on socioeconomic, geographic, or demographic characteristics
The split between users who protect their personal data online and users who do not
questions.rationale: EK IOC-1.C.2 defines the term almost word for word: the digital divide refers to differing access to computing devices and the Internet, based on socioeconomic, geographic, or demographic characteristics — option C. A confuses the divide with hardware performance differences. B swaps in a connection-technology distinction the CED never draws. D describes safe-computing behavior (Topic 5.6 territory), not differing access.

questions.stem: Every home in Rosa's city neighborhood can order fast broadband, and most of her classmates have it. Rosa's family cannot afford the monthly bill, so she drafts essays on her phone. Per EK IOC-1.C.3, which statement about the digital divide fits this case?
options: It can affect an individual even when the surrounding group is well connected
It applies only when an entire region or population lacks access at the same time
It cannot apply here, since the neighborhood's infrastructure supports fast service
It applies only to comparisons between nations, so one student's situation falls outside it
questions.rationale: EK IOC-1.C.3 states the digital divide can affect both groups and individuals, so one under-connected student inside a well-connected group is squarely inside the concept — option A. B keeps the group half of the EK and drops the individual half, the exact error the item tests. C confuses available infrastructure with actual access; Rosa's barrier is socioeconomic (EK IOC-1.C.2), not technical. D repeats the countries-only myth that EK IOC-1.C.1's within-country characteristics refute.

questions.stem: Which of the following statements about the digital divide are true? I. It raises issues of equity, access, and influence. II. Those issues arise both globally and locally. III. It can be narrowed only by the actions of governments.
options: I and III only
II and III only
I, II, and III
I and II only
questions.rationale: Statements I and II restate EK IOC-1.C.4: the digital divide raises issues of equity, access, and influence, both globally and locally. Statement III contradicts EK IOC-1.C.5, which says the divide is affected by the actions of individuals, organizations, AND governments — so every option containing III (A, B, and C) fails. D — I and II only — is correct; students pick A or B when they assume closing the divide is purely a matter of national policy.

questions.stem: A town's Internet gap narrows after three separate efforts: a retired teacher runs free tablet classes, the regional ISP introduces a $10-per-month plan, and the state funds fiber lines to outlying farms. Per EK IOC-1.C.5, what do these efforts show?
options: Only the state's fiber funding truly affected the divide; the other two efforts are unrelated to it
The divide shrinks automatically over time as technology becomes cheaper for everyone
The divide is affected by the actions of individuals, organizations, and governments
Organizations act on the divide only after a government regulation requires them to do so
questions.rationale: The three efforts map one-to-one onto EK IOC-1.C.5's three levels: an individual (the retired teacher), an organization (the ISP), and a government (the state) — option C. A dismisses two levels the EK explicitly names; the classes and the low-cost plan attack skills and affordability barriers directly. B credits an automatic trend, while the stem describes three deliberate actions. D adds a requirement the CED never states — the ISP acted without any mandate in the stem.

questions.stem: A classmate argues: 'The digital divide means the gap between rich and poor countries, so it is not an issue inside a fully developed nation.' Which response best applies EK IOC-1.C.1?
options: The claim is correct — within a developed nation, differences in access are too small to matter
The claim is incorrect — access also varies with socioeconomic, geographic, and demographic characteristics inside one country
The claim is correct — the term applies only to comparisons involving at least two nations
The claim is incorrect — the divide actually refers only to differences within a single country
questions.rationale: EK IOC-1.C.1 says Internet access varies between socioeconomic, geographic, and demographic characteristics AS WELL AS between countries — the divide runs inside nations too, so B is the CED-aligned correction. A and C both endorse the countries-only reading the EK contradicts, and EK IOC-1.C.4 reinforces the point by raising equity, access, and influence issues both globally and locally. D overcorrects into the opposite error, erasing the between-countries clause that is genuinely part of the EK.

questions.stem: A city audit finds residents over 75 go online at half the rate of younger residents — even in buildings where every unit includes free high-speed broadband. Which characteristic best explains this gap?
options: Demographic — age-related differences shape who uses the access that exists
Socioeconomic — the older residents cannot afford the service being offered
Geographic — the buildings sit in an area with weak Internet infrastructure
Between countries — the older residents grew up under other nations' networks
questions.rationale: Free broadband in every unit removes infrastructure and price as explanations, isolating a demographic characteristic — age — as what shapes use of the access that exists (EK IOC-1.C.1, C.2): option A. B is contradicted by the service being free to residents. C is contradicted by younger residents in the same buildings connecting at full rates. D misapplies the between-countries clause to an age cohort living in one city; where residents grew up is not what the clause compares.

guide.title: Topic 5.2 — Digital Divide — Teacher Guide
guide.subtitle: Big Idea 5: Impact of Computing · Topic 5.2 · 1 class period + assessment block
overview: Topic 5.2 puts a name and a structure on something many students have lived: the same assignment, the same app, the same opportunity costing different people different amounts. Students learn the CED's definition of the digital divide, the characteristics access varies along (socioeconomic, geographic, demographic — and between countries), the scale rule (groups AND individuals), the issues raised (equity, access, influence — globally and locally), and the three levels of action that move the gap (individuals, organizations, governments).

The through-line is the bell ringer: Maya with home fiber and Jordan with a 2 GB phone plan receive the identical homework, and students inventory every step where it costs Jordan more. Every later slide pins CED vocabulary onto costs the class found itself, and the worked table turns the skill around: given five scenarios, diagnose the characteristic and name an actor who could narrow the gap.
los: LO IOC-1.C — Describe issues that contribute to the digital divide.
eks: EK IOC-1.C.1 — Internet access varies between socioeconomic, geographic, and demographic characteristics, as well as between countries.
EK IOC-1.C.2 — The "digital divide" refers to differing access to computing devices and the Internet, based on socioeconomic, geographic, or demographic characteristics.
EK IOC-1.C.3 — The digital divide can affect both groups and individuals.
EK IOC-1.C.4 — The digital divide raises issues of equity, access, and influence, both globally and locally.
EK IOC-1.C.5 — The digital divide is affected by the actions of individuals, organizations, and governments.
materials: Printed Topic 5.2 quiz (student copies) for the assessment block.
Optional: the Exercise 1 access-audit log (Maple Hollow) printed for early finishers.
prereqs: Topic 5.1 (Beneficial and Harmful Effects): students should already frame innovations as having effects beyond their purpose — 5.2 narrows to the effect of unequal access itself. The bell ringer assumes no prior knowledge of the term 'digital divide.'

pacing.focus: The divide's definition and dimensions, then who it affects and who can act (LO IOC-1.C)

segments.segment: QOTD (BI5/T5.2/Q1) + bell ringer (one assignment, two students, unequal costs)
What the divide is: vocab, dimensions, only-poor-countries misconception (EK IOC-1.C.1–C.3)
Stop and Think: name the dimensions
Who it affects, who can act + diagnose-the-gap table (EK IOC-1.C.3–C.5)
Deep dive: more than on or off (optional enrichment)
Stop and Think: who moves the gap?

pacing.label: Flex / Assessment block — no new deck (uses the Topic Quiz)
pacing.focus: Not a numbered prep day. Run the Topic 5.2 quiz as the opener of the next class (doubles as spaced retrieval). The quiz ships as its own document, so no new deck or notes are needed.

misconceptions.heading: “The digital divide is only about poor countries”
misconceptions.myth: The divide describes the gap between wealthy and developing nations; inside a rich, wired country, everyone has roughly the same access.
misconceptions.reality: EK IOC-1.C.1 lists between-countries variation as one clause among four: access also varies with socioeconomic, geographic, and demographic characteristics inside a single country, and EK IOC-1.C.4 raises the equity, access, and influence issues both globally AND locally. Maya and Jordan share a zip code and still sit on opposite sides.

misconceptions.heading: “The divide is just an income gap”
misconceptions.myth: Digital divide is a synonym for poverty — whoever has money has access, so fixing income fixes access.
misconceptions.reality: Income is one axis of three (plus countries). A high-income farm that no provider serves has a geographic gap; a senior with a free tablet and free Wi-Fi who never connects has a demographic one. Exam scenarios are built to punish the everything-is-money diagnosis — the characteristic must match the evidence.

misconceptions.heading: “Only governments can close it”
misconceptions.myth: The divide is infrastructure policy, so only state action counts; individuals and companies are bystanders.
misconceptions.reality: EK IOC-1.C.5 credits three levels by name: individuals (teaching a neighbor, donating a device), organizations (low-cost plans, lending hotspots), and governments (buildout, subsidies). The worked table and Q5 of the quiz both hinge on recognizing all three — 'only the government action mattered' is a scripted distractor.

support: Give the diagnosis frame as a sentence starter: 'In this scenario, access differs by ______ (characteristic); it affects ______ (a group / an individual); and ______ (individual / organization / government) could ______.' Students fill it for every worked-table row before free-form answers.
Anchor every new term back to the two bell-ringer students: 'Is this a Maya fact or a Jordan fact — and which characteristic made it different?'
extension: Have students research one real digital-inclusion effort at each of the three action levels (a volunteer program, a company or nonprofit program, a government program), then classify which characteristic each one targets and who it leaves out.
Ask deep-dive students to design an 'access audit' for their own town: list the free public access points, their hours, and travel times, then write one paragraph on which residents the map fails.

discussion: Is home Internet access now as essential as electricity or running water? If yes, who should be obligated to provide it — and who should pay?

The CED says the divide raises issues of influence. Whose experiences and opinions are missing from the online spaces where decisions get made — and what does the Internet 'know' less about as a result?
A school goes fully paperless to be modern. Using today's two students, argue when a technology upgrade quietly widens a divide — and name one policy that would prevent it.
guide.answerKeyNote: Each question's full rationale is on the KEY copy of the quiz (print it with the answer-key option). The short answer key below is auto-derived from the live quiz block, so it can never drift from the student questions.
